1:
"When they drew nigh unto Jerusalem, and were come to Bethphage, unto the Mount of Olives, then sent Jesus two disciples." The witnesses gather around three things: the historical setting, the deliberate staging of a humble kingship, and a rich patristic allegory.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the scene. Bethphage — "the house of unripe figs" (Robertson, Clarke, Ellicott) — lay on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives, a little nearer Jericho than Bethany; Remigius adds it was "a small village of the priests," a Sabbath-day's mile from the city. Barnes and Ellicott reconstruct the journey: the company had climbed the nineteen-mile desert road from Jericho, reached Bethany "six days before the Passover" (John 12:1), rested over the Sabbath, and set out at the dawn of Palm Sunday. The two disciples are unnamed in every Gospel, though Ellicott and Henry guess Peter and John from the parallel errand of Luke 22:8.

For the Reformed and Puritan voices the whole act is a calculated declaration. Calvin sees Christ, "very near the end of his course," resolving "to commence his reign openly on earth," yet doing so on a borrowed ass without saddle or trappings — "a magnificent display, truly!" — precisely to show that his kingdom "does not resemble earthly kingdoms." Only the prophecy of Zechariah 9:9 keeps the scene from absurdity; without it "we shall never have any relish for this history." Henry presses the same paradox: the provision was "poor and ordinary, and such as bespoke his kingdom to be not of this world" — "once in his life he rode in triumph; and it was now when he went... to suffer and die." Chrysostom too finds a lesson in contentment, that it is enough "to employ an ass," and challenges the Jews to name another king who entered Jerusalem so.

The fathers — Remigius, Origen, Pseudo-Chrysostom, Jerome, and Hilary — read the two beasts as the two peoples. The ass, "under the yoke" and broken, is the synagogue long accustomed to the Law; the unbroken colt is the Gentiles, the Jewish nation being "towards God the mother of the Gentiles" (Jerome). The animals "tied" picture humanity "bound in the chain of diabolic error" until the apostles, sent like the two disciples, "loose them" by preaching (Pseudo-Chrysostom). The two messengers are variously the two Testaments, the two precepts of charity, or the two orders of preachers — to the circumcision and to the Gentiles (Remigius, citing Galatians 2:8). Finally Augustine handles the harmony: Matthew alone names the she-ass (John omits it), a difference he traces to Matthew's Hebrew original and the freedom of the Septuagint — a reminder "that we should not suppose that the truth is secured in sacred sounds," but in the matter conveyed.
2:
"Go into the village over against you, and... ye shall find an ass tied, and a colt with her: loose them, and bring them unto me." The witnesses divide between the historical detail and the display of Christ's foreknowledge.

The grammatical-historical commentators locate the village and weigh the animals. It was either Bethany or, if nearer the city, Bethphage itself (Ellicott); Gill reports a Jewish tradition naming it Nob, "a city of the priests" within sight of Jerusalem, and even a rabbinic admission that Jesus took an ass from there. Mark, Luke, and John name only the colt — "a young ass" (John), one "on which man had never sat" (Mark 11:2; Luke 19:30) — while Matthew, following Zechariah's Hebrew, names both. The choice of beast carries dignity, not poverty: Barnes shows that in Judea, where horses were kept chiefly for war, "kings and princes commonly rode" on the ass and mule in times of peace — so Solomon at his enthronement (1 Kings 1:33), and the judges' sons before him (Judges 10:4; 12:14). To ride thus was "the appropriate way in which a king should ride," and "in which... the King of Zion should enter into his capital."

Lightfoot, from the Talmud, turns the borrowing into a proof. The rabbis held that "whosoever transgresses against the will of the owner is called a robber" — one who hires an ass for the plain and turns to the hills. Here, then, "this of our Saviour appears to be a miracle, not a robbery," that without "any agreement or terms" the ass was led away and the owner "satisfied with these bare words, 'The Lord hath need of him.'"

Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the verse a demonstration of divinity. Christ did not need the ass "to relieve the fatigue of traveling," for the distance was short; rather, "as kings are wont to ascend their chariots, from which they may be easily seen," he "intended to turn the eyes of the people on himself." To steady the disciples' obedience he foretells two things — that at the village's entrance they "will find an ass," and that none "will hinder them" once they answer that the Lord has need. "In this way he proved his Divinity," Calvin insists; "both to know absent matters, and to bend the hearts of men to compliance, belonged to God alone." He pointedly dismisses the patristic allegory of the two beasts as "ridiculous," holding (with the other Gospels and Zechariah's parallelism) that only the colt was ridden. Clarke agrees that Christ "takes every opportunity to convince his disciples that nothing was hidden from him," disposing "the owner's heart" to consent.
3:
"And if any man say ought unto you, ye shall say, The Lord hath need of them; and straightway he will send them." The verse turns almost entirely on the title "the Lord," and the grammatical-historical school weighs its force.

Ellicott lays out three possible senses. "The Lord" may be (1) "in the highest sense... equivalent to Jehovah," as though the beasts were claimed for God's service; (2) "Christ in the special sense in which He was spoken of as 'the Lord' by His disciples"; or (3) merely "the Master whom the disciples owned." The third he all but excludes, "for the words involve a claim to more than common authority, and the claim is recognised at once." Marshalling the many places where the Gospels call Jesus "the Lord," Ellicott settles on (2) as "most in accordance with the customary language of the disciples" — and, "on the very probable assumption that the owners of the colt were... themselves disciples, they would recognise the full import of the words... and obey without hesitation."

Robertson presses the point further. This is "the only time in Matthew where the words ho kurios are applied to Jesus" apart from the doubtful 28:6. He traces the word's weight in the wider world: in the Septuagint and the papyri it names the master of a slave (Matthew 10:24), the emperor (Acts 13:27), and God himself (Matthew 11:25); in Egypt men spoke of "the Lord Serapis," and even Herod was hailed as "Lord King." Against that background "the Christians boldly claimed the word for Christ," and here "Jesus is represented as using it with reference to himself," accepting the title his disciples were already giving him.

Barnes, more cautiously, takes "Lord" as simply "master," a "title of high respect" (so Matthew 10:24; Ephesians 6:6; 1 Peter 3:6), but agrees the owners were "probably acquainted with the miracles of Jesus, and favourably disposed," for he had lately raised Lazarus and "most of the people regarded him as the Messiah." Clarke holds both notes together: "the proprietor of all things hath need of them," for Christ, "continually humbling himself," is yet "ever giving proofs of his almighty power, that the belief of his divinity may be established." The shared sense is that the word, slight on the surface, carries a quiet claim to lordship the willing owner instantly honours.
4:
"All this was done, that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophet, saying," The verse is Matthew's fulfilment-formula, and the witnesses examine the citation and the disciples' delayed understanding.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the quotation as composite. Robertson notes that "the first line is from Isaiah 62:11, the rest from Zechariah 9:9," and that — as John makes plain (John 12:14ff.) — "Jesus did not quote the passage himself"; it is rather the Evangelist's own comment, "not Christ's intention to fulfil the prophecy, simply that his conduct did fulfil it." Barnes and Clarke confirm that the prophecy "was always, by the Jews, applied to the Messiah," and Clarke draws the double purpose of inspired prediction: "to show the truth of prophecy in general," and "to designate Christ as the person intended." Gill makes the same point negatively — Christ rode "not because of the distance" or "because he was weary," for he was a seasoned traveller, but to fulfil what was written.

Ellicott adds the telling detail from John: at the time "the disciples did not understand its significance as connected with the prophecy" (John 12:16). "The purpose lay in the mind of their Master, not in theirs" — and it is "significant," he observes, that neither Mark nor Luke alludes to the prophecy at all. The fulfilment, in other words, was not staged by the participants for effect; it was wrought by the deliberate will of Christ and recognised only afterward. Ellicott observes that the Evangelist here returns to the very formula of Matthew 1:22 — literally, "all this has come to pass" — his own comment on the act rather than a word spoken in the scene.

One Eastern voice, Cyril of Jerusalem, stands apart, turning from the entry to the gift of the Spirit: he reads "the third hour" of the crucifixion as the hour when grace was later poured out, citing Joel's prophecy, "I will pour forth of My Spirit upon all flesh" (Joel 2:28) — a catechetical excursus on Pentecost rather than a comment on the verse, but a reminder that the King who enters in meekness is the same who bestows the Spirit.
5:
"Tell ye the daughter of Zion, Behold, thy King cometh unto thee, meek, and sitting upon an ass." The prophecy itself draws the fullest comment, the witnesses dwelling on the daughter of Zion, the meek peace-king, and the composite citation.

On "the daughter of Zion" the grammatical-historical and Reformed voices agree it means not the buildings but the people — "the inhabitants thereof, the Jewish synagogue," and especially "the elect of God among them, those that embraced the true Messiah" (Gill); Wesley likewise glosses it "the inhabitants of Jerusalem." All note the citation is composite and freely quoted: "the first words... from Isaiah 62:11, the rest from Zechariah 9:9" (Wesley, Robertson), the Hebrew's "ass... even a colt" being plain parallelism, not two beasts (Robertson, Calvin).

The heart of the verse is the meekness of the King. Calvin contrasts him with "earthly kings, whose apparel is very magnificent and costly," for the prophet marks him by poverty, riding a borrowed beast "of the common people." Cyril of Jerusalem makes this the very test of identity: many kings come in purple, guarded by spearmen, in golden chariots, but the prophet gives "a sign peculiar to the King" — "meek, and riding upon an ass" — and "Jesus alone of kings sat upon an unyoked foal, entering into Jerusalem with acclamations as a king." Wesley glories in the lowliness: "the Prince of Peace did not take a horse, a warlike animal," though "he will ride on that by and by" (Revelation 19:11); the mean attitude is "for the honour of his humility, and for the utter confusion of all worldly pomp." Clarke calls the entry "the triumph of humility over pride and worldly grandeur... of meekness over rage and malice."

Ellicott reads the prophecy's larger sweep: the ideal King comes not with "chariot and horse and battle bow" but "as a prince of peace," reviving "the lowlier pageantry of the days of the Judges" (Judges 10:4; 12:14), yet wielding a dominion "from sea to sea" (Zechariah 9:10). He finds the act two-edged — "in part an apparent concession to the fevered expectations of His disciples and the multitude; in part... a protest" against them, for the kingdom would prove "not of this world" (John 18:36). Lightfoot adds that the day completed a double prophecy — Zechariah's, and the law's taking of the paschal lamb on the tenth day (Exodus 12:3) — and recounts the rabbis' puzzle that Daniel's Messiah comes "with the clouds of heaven" while Zechariah's comes "upon an ass": he comes in the clouds as judge, says Lightfoot, but on the ass "not because you are, but because he is, good."
6:
"And the disciples went, and did as Jesus commanded them." Though brief, the verse is read by the witnesses as a small model of obedient faith.

Gill supplies the topography from Mark: the colt was "tied to the door without, in a place where two ways met" — the house standing "just at the entrance of the village," the beast fastened in the open street, so that it was "a public place," easily seen and not to be "taken away without being observed," as indeed its owners observed (Mark 11:4). Ellicott fills in the same scene from Mark and Luke "more graphically": the disciples' finding of the colt, "the question asked by the owner and the by-standers why they did it," and their answer in the very words they had been given, "The Lord hath need of them"; "they returned with the ass and the colt, and then the procession began."

Calvin, for the Reformed, makes obedience the verse's center. The disciples' compliance is "mentioned with commendation," he says, because the task was far from easy: "the influence of Christ was not so great, that his name alone would be sufficient to produce an impression on unknown persons," and "there was reason to fear that they would be blamed for theft." That they "make no reply, but proceed readily towards that place to which he has ordered them," relying simply "on his command and promise," is "a proof of the deference which they paid to their Master."

From this Calvin presses the application: "Let us also learn by their example to press forward through every kind of difficulty, so as to render to the Lord the obedience which he demands from us; for he will remove obstacles, and open up a path, and will not permit our endeavors to be unavailing." The verse thus becomes a quiet picture of faith acting on a bare word and finding the word made good — the whole errand, Calvin concludes, "directed by God." It is the same lesson with which the chapter opened: what Christ commands, he also enables, and the believer who obeys a plain word need not be deterred by the obstacles that seem to stand in the way.
7:
"And brought the ass and the colt, and put on them their clothes, and set him thereon." The witnesses divide between a textual/harmony question and the homage signified by the garments.

On the harmony the grammatical-historical commentators are agreed. Mark and Luke say Jesus rode the colt (Mark 11:7; Luke 19:35), and Matthew does not contradict them: "them" in "set him thereon" refers to the garments thrown over the colt, not to two animals (Robertson). Ellicott concurs — "our Lord rode on the colt, and the ass followed, or went along by His side." Clarke notes the textual variant: against epano auton ("upon them") the Codex Bezae and several Old Latin copies read ep' auton ("upon him," the colt), "most likely... the true reading; for we can scarcely suppose that he rode upon both by turns... or that he rode upon both at once." Robertson observes the garments were the outer himatia, and that Jesus "took his seat" (an ingressive aorist) upon them.

The act of laying the garments down is read as homage. Clarke explains it was "a custom observed by the people when they found that God had appointed a man to the kingdom," citing Jehu, beneath whom the captains spread their garments on the steps, crying "Jehu is king" (2 Kings 9:13); thus the disciples, having no saddle, "set him thereon," "acknowledging him to be their king."

Clarke also records, without fully endorsing, the older allegory: "the ass that was tied up was an emblem of the Jews bound under the yoke of the law; and the colt that had not been tied represented the Gentiles who were not under the law," Christ's sitting on both signifying "his subjecting the Jews and the Gentiles to the sway of his evangelical scepter" — to which he adds the careful proviso, "He who can receive this saying, let him receive it." The grammatical-historical reading and the patristic allegory thus stand side by side, the former preferring the plain sense of a single colt, the latter hearing in the two beasts the gathering of two peoples.
8:
"And a very great multitude spread their garments in the way; others cut down branches from the trees, and strawed them in the way." The witnesses describe the homage, its sources in the crowd, and its symbolism.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the crowd and the gestures. The throng was a confluence — part come up with Jesus from Jericho and Galilee, part streaming from Bethany "excited by the recent resurrection of Lazarus" (John 12:17), and part pouring out from Jerusalem with palm-branches to meet him (John 12:13). Spreading garments was "a recognised act of homage to a king" — so the captains beneath Jehu (2 Kings 9:13; so Wesley too), and, Ellicott adds, Agamemnon walking on costly carpets in Aeschylus. Barnes expounds the palm at length: "an emblem of joy and victory," used by Jews and Romans alike as "a symbol of peace" (1 Maccabees 13:51; Revelation 7:9), abundant about Jericho, "the city of palm-trees." Robertson, noting "the most part of the multitude" did this, traces "the growing enthusiasm of the crowd" through the change of tenses, as they picked up the garments after the colt had passed and spread them again.

Lightfoot supplies the Jewish ritual background. The people did not, he argues, strew garments and boughs directly "under the feet of the ass to be trod on" — that "might have thrown down the rider" — but "by the wayside they made little tents and tabernacles of clothes and boughs, according to the custom of the feast of Tabernacles," carrying the lulab, the "young branches of palms" prescribed in Leviticus 23:40. Clarke adds the rabbinic legends gathered round the ass — that it was the colt "created in the twilight of the sixth day," ridden by Abraham and Moses, "and... on which the Messiah shall ride."

Calvin, for the Reformed, weighs the meaning. The scene "might be thought... a game of little children," that "obscure persons" should bestow "the empty title of king"; but "they did this in good earnest," and "Christ looked upon them as fit heralds of his kingdom" — even now, Calvin notes, he "commissions from the heavenly throne obscure men, by whom his majesty is celebrated in a despicable manner." He judges the branches were cut not "in accordance with an ancient and solemn rite," but "by a sudden movement of the Spirit," the rest of the multitude imitating what a few began.
9:
"Hosanna to the Son of David; Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord; Hosanna in the highest." The acclamation draws careful comment on its words, its source, and its confession of the Messiah.

On the meaning the witnesses agree. "Hosanna" is the Hebrew imperative "Save now, we beseech thee" (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, Ellicott), drawn into liturgical use from Psalm 118:25–26 within the "great Hallel" (Psalms 113–118) sung at the feasts. Clarke connects it with the Tabernacles ritual, when the people circled the altar bearing branches and, on the eighth day, cried the "hosanna rabba," the Great Hosanna. "Son of David," all note, names the Messiah: by it the crowd "were now proclaiming Jesus as the Messiah and he let them do it" (Robertson). "Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord" is likewise from the Psalm — coming "by the authority of the Lord" (Barnes) — and "Hosanna in the highest" invokes salvation from "the highest heaven," echoing the angels' song at the Nativity (Luke 2:14).

Ellicott gathers the full range of cries from the four Gospels — "Blessed be the King," "Blessed be the kingdom of our father David" (Mark), "Peace in heaven, and glory in the highest" (Luke) — and sets against them the silent grief of Christ, who would not rebuke the shout at the Pharisees' bidding (Luke 19:39) yet wept over the city's coming fall (Luke 19:41).

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the cry as inspired prayer. The words are "not... rashly bestowed on Christ"; the people "followed with reverence the form of prayer which the Holy Spirit had prescribed to the whole Church by the mouth of the Prophet." Though the Psalm spoke first of David's kingdom, "he principally looks... to the eternal succession" — Christ's. So when we pray "Thy kingdom come," Calvin says, "is conveyed the same thing which is expressed more clearly in the psalm." That "mean and uneducated men" thus hailed the kingdom proves "this doctrine was at that time well known." Wesley adds that the multitude cried "probably from a Divine impulse, for certainly most of them understood not the words they uttered" — and that Christ, who had till now "restrained all public tokens of honour," suffered them, "that they might be a public testimony against their wickedness, who in four or five days after cried out, Crucify him."
10:
"And when he was come into Jerusalem, all the city was moved, saying, Who is this?" The witnesses dwell on the city's commotion, its ignorance set against the crowd's confession, and Christ's entry into the temple.

The grammatical-historical commentators render the stir vividly. The verb (eseisthe) means the city "was shaken" as by an earthquake (Robertson, Clarke) — "even Jerusalem, frozen with religious formalism... was stirred with popular enthusiasm as by a mighty wind." Ellicott notes it was "the beginning of the Paschal week," the city "filled with pilgrims of many lands," to whom this triumph "was a strange prelude to the usual order of the feast," so they asked what it meant. Their question "Who is this?" exposes, says Henry's tradition and Jerome alike, the ignorance of the holy city — "the Holy One unknown in the holy city" — while the common multitude knew him better than "the Scribes and Pharisees, and rulers." Origen hears beneath it the Psalm, "Who is this King of glory?" (Psalm 24:8).

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the public character of the scene. Matthew records the commotion "to inform us that the transaction did not take place secretly... and that the priests and scribes were not ignorant of it." That his enemies "abstained from opposing it" was "not because they treated him with contempt, but rather because they were restrained by secret fear; for God had struck them with such alarm, that they dare not make any attempt." Yet the city's question betrays "the careless indifference" of its inhabitants, over against "the piety of those who have just reached it."

The fathers turn at once to what follows — Christ entering the temple. Pseudo-Chrysostom finds it "the part of a good Son to haste to His Father's house," and "of a good physician" to "first apply himself to the source of the sickness"; for "when the priesthood is sound, the whole Church flourishes, but if it is corrupt, faith is impaired." Raban marks the timing: the entry was "five days before the passover," answering the law's command that the paschal lamb be "taken into the house" on the tenth day (Exodus 12:3) — "the correspondence between the Old and New Testaments, not only in things but in seasons." So the King, hailed without and unknown within, comes to purify his Father's house.
11:
"And the multitude said, This is Jesus the prophet of Nazareth of Galilee." The witnesses read the answer as a true but incomplete confession, marred by a mistaken epithet.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices weigh both halves. Gill observes that "the common people... knew Christ better than the inhabitants of Jerusalem, the Scribes and Pharisees, and rulers of the people"; the crowd's testimony, however humble, outran the city's blank ignorance. To call him "the Prophet" was, says Clarke, to name "that prophet whom Moses spoke of" — "I will raise them up a prophet... like unto thee" (Deuteronomy 18:18) — so that "every expression of the multitude plainly intimated that they fully received our blessed Lord as the promised Messiah." Jerome makes the same point: "they begin with the lesser that they may come to the greater," hailing him as the Prophet "like to" Moses, the Greek marking the title with the definite article. Hitherto, Henry's tradition adds, Christ had been known chiefly as a prophet; only now do they attend him as a King.

Yet the witnesses note the flaw in the confession — "of Nazareth." This "helped to confirm some in their prejudices against him," for it seemed to disqualify him as Messiah. Wesley meets the objection: "if he was of Nazareth, he could not be the Messiah. But they who earnestly desired to know the truth would not stumble thereat; for upon inquiry... they would find he was not of Nazareth, but Bethlehem." Clarke turns the scene toward its bitter sequel, marvelling at the fickleness of the crowd: "How strange is it that these same people... should, about five days after, change their hosannas for, Away with him! crucify him!" The verse thus holds together a genuine, Spirit-prompted recognition and the half-knowledge that, untaught and unstable, would soon give way before the rulers' malice. The witnesses thus make the verse a mirror: the despised crowd sees more clearly than the learned city, yet even their sight is dim, mixing genuine homage with the error that would let them be swept, within the week, into the opposite cry. True confession of Christ, the lesson runs, must be founded not on the mood of the moment but on the searching of the Scriptures, which point past Nazareth to Bethlehem and past the prophet to the King.
12:
"And Jesus went into the temple of God, and cast out all them that sold and bought in the temple, and overthrew the tables of the moneychangers, and the seats of them that sold doves." The witnesses treat the act on three fronts: its relation to the earlier cleansing, the nature of the traffic, and the authority it displayed.

The grammatical-historical commentators reconstruct the trade. It was held in the outer Court of the Gentiles, "the least sacred part of the temple" (Barnes, whose long description maps the whole sacred enclosure): there pilgrims bought oxen, sheep, and doves for sacrifice, and the money-changers exchanged foreign coin for the Jewish half-shekel of the temple tax (Exodus 30:13). Lightfoot notes a "constant market" in the temple "shops," swollen now by the nearing Passover, and tells the tale of Bava Ben Buta importing three thousand sheep. Ellicott explains how the priests "found their profit" by charging rent and how the changers took "the usual agio," and — lest we judge too harshly — recalls St Paul's cathedral in Elizabeth's day, where "mules and horses laden with market produce were led through... as a matter of every-day occurrence." On the doves Ellicott notes a sharper edge: at the first cleansing Christ had merely bidden the dealers depart (John 2:16), but now, "as if indignant at their return," he overturns their seats too.

Most agree this was a second cleansing. Chrysostom infers from the differing replies that John's account (John 2:14) was a separate, earlier event, and presses "a heavier charge against the Jews," who, reproved twice, "continued in their trafficking." Robertson marvels "how short a time the work of reformers lasts"; Barnes and Ellicott add (from Mark) that the purging was likely completed the day after the entry.

For the Puritan and Reformed voices the deed is an act of sovereign authority. Henry calls it "the only act of regal authority and coercive power that Christ did in the days of his flesh," and makes it the pattern of all reformation — "the eye must be upon the scripture," reducing "corrupted ordinances... to their primitive institution." Calvin agrees Christ here "declared himself to be both King and High Priest, who presided over the temple," but warns "lest any private individual should think himself entitled to act in the same manner": the godly must "burn with grief" at the church's pollutions, yet, lacking "public authority," must "oppose by their tongue... those vices which they cannot remedy with their hands." Wesley notes the sharper words this second time, the trade being in "the outer court... where the Gentiles used to worship."
13:
"And said unto them, It is written, My house shall be called the house of prayer; but ye have made it a den of thieves." The witnesses examine the two prophetic texts and what each declares about the temple.

All identify the citation as a free combination: "My house shall be called the house of prayer" from Isaiah 56:7, and "a den of thieves" from Jeremiah 7:11 (Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley). Henry draws out the first: "all the ceremonial institutions were intended to be subservient to moral duties; the house of sacrifices was to be a house of prayer, for that was the substance and soul of all those services." Calvin presses the universal scope of Isaiah's vision: it foretells "the calling of the Gentiles," that the temple should be "a house of prayer for all nations" — never literally fulfilled by all nations going up to Jerusalem, but realised when "out of Zion went forth the law" (Isaiah 2:3; Micah 4:2) and the living waters flowed out from the temple to water the world (Ezekiel 47:9; Zechariah 14:8).

The "den of thieves" the witnesses read with pictorial force. Palestine "swarmed with bands of outlaw brigands" who, like David in Adullam (1 Samuel 22:1), "haunted the lime-stone caverns of Judaea" (Ellicott, Clarke); their wrangling over booty "was reproduced in the Temple, and mingled with the Hallelujahs of the Levites." Robertson adds the offence was "charging exorbitant prices." Jeremiah's word, Calvin notes, struck at "hypocrites, who, through confidence in" the temple "allowed themselves greater liberty in sinning" — making "dissembled piety... the cloak and cover of iniquity" (so Henry, who calls robbing God the worst theft, Malachi 3:8).

Barnes asks why the traders submitted, and answers: they were "overawed by his authority," reproved by "their own consciences," backed by "the people generally" who held him Messiah, and aware that "a prophet had a right to... order the various affairs relating to external worship." Mark and Luke add that the chief priests, stung, "sought how they might destroy him" but feared the people (Mark 11:18). Clarke turns the scene to the church of every age, where those "who enjoy the revenues of the Church, and neglect the service of it, are thieves and robbers in more senses than one."
14:
"And the blind and the lame came to him in the temple; and he healed them." The witnesses read the healings as the temple's true use and as a sign of the Messiah.

Henry's tradition and Clarke set the contrast: having driven out the buyers and sellers, "he invited the blind and lame into it." "The temple was profane and abused when it was made a market-place, but it was graced and honoured when it was made an hospital; to be doing good in God's house... better becomes it than to be getting money there." Clarke makes it a test of any congregation: "the Church or chapel in which the blind and the lame are not healed has no Christ in it, and is not worthy of attendance."

Ellicott takes up the old difficulty of 2 Samuel 5:8. The Septuagint reading would make "the blind and the lame" excluded "from the house of the Lord," but "the words in italics are not in the Hebrew," and there is "no evidence from Jewish writers that the blind and the lame were ever... excluded." What the passages truly set side by side, he says, is "the contrast between the hasty, passionate words of the conquering king" David and "the tender compassion of the Son of David, to whom the blind and the lame were objects, not of antipathy, but pity." They thronged the temple gates as beggars (so Acts 3:2), and now followed "the great Healer" inside.

Calvin, for the Reformed, fastens on the evidential weight: "that the authority which Christ had claimed for himself... might not be suspected of rashness, he supported it by miracles." By healing he proclaimed "that the rights and honor of Messiah truly belonged to him; for by these marks the prophets describe him" (so Barnes cites Isaiah 35:6). The cures were as it were God's own endorsement from heaven of the praises the people had raised. And here again Calvin presses his caution: it is "not every one of the people who is called to imitate this action of Christ," lest he "inconsiderately raise himself to the throne of the Messiah." The works, says Henry, "testified of him more than the hosannas," and fulfilled the promise "that the glory of the latter house should be greater than the glory of the former."
15:
"And when the chief priests and scribes saw the wonderful things that he did, and the children crying in the temple... they were sore displeased." The witnesses dwell on the rulers' indignation and on the children's praise.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the priests' motive. They "could not deny" the miracles "to be true," says Henry's tradition, "and therefore were cut to the heart"; Clarke sharpens it — they were "incensed" that the temple had been purified, "a work they should have done themselves," and "strange as it may appear, the priesthood itself, in all corrupt times, has been ever the most forward to prevent a reform in the Church," fearing "a reformer would find them no better than money-changers." Both the miracles and the shouts galled them; the chief priests, Ellicott notes, were "the heads of the twenty-four courses" together with Annas and Caiaphas.

The children — "the boys," strictly (Robertson, Ellicott), perhaps seven to fourteen — had "caught the enthusiasm of the crowd." Lightfoot supplies the custom: "children, from their first infancy, were taught to manage the bundles, to shake them, and in shaking, to sing Hosanna"; "so soon as a little child can be taught to manage a bundle, he is bound to carry one." So their cry was no novelty in itself, "only they were exasperated, because a person was celebrated against whom they had a rooted hatred" (Clarke).

Calvin, for the Reformed, locates the deeper grievance in wounded authority. The priests "contended for their authority," and it was "no slight diminution of their power, if the people were at liberty to bestow on Christ the title of King." They judged it "foolish and unreasonable, that the people should confer the title of Messiah on one whom they do not treat with any respect" — for if they had done their duty as "the messengers of the Lord of hosts" (Malachi 2:7), they should have led the people to him. But since "they had basely extinguished the light of truth," Christ replies that their efforts to suppress it are vain: "God will sooner give mouths and tongues" to stones "than allow the kingdom of His Son to be without witnesses." It is, Calvin adds, "no slight consolation," that the malice of the great cannot silence the praise of Christ.
16:
"Hearest thou what these say? And Jesus saith unto them, Yea; have ye never read, Out of the mouth of babes and sucklings thou hast perfected praise?" The witnesses focus on the rebuke and the Psalm it draws upon.

The priests' question, Ellicott explains, was a surprise sprung on them: they "had probably remained in the Temple, and had not heard the Hosannas... raised on the Mount of Olives," so the children's shouts startled them, and they "turned to the Teacher and asked whether He accepted them" — whether he had really "entered the Temple claiming to be the expected Christ." Christ's "Yea" removes "even the suspicion of the contrary."

His answer cites Psalm 8:2. The commentators note the quotation follows "the LXX translation" — "thou hast perfected praise," where the Hebrew reads "thou hast ordained strength" (Barnes, Robertson) — but "the point of the quotation," that "children might offer praise to God," stands in both. Robertson observes the verb "perfected" (katertiso) is the same used of mending nets in Matthew 4:21, and calls the citation "a stinging rebuke." Clarke adds that this Psalm is applied to Christ thrice elsewhere (1 Corinthians 15:27; Ephesians 1:22; Hebrews 2:6), proving it "merely a prophetic psalm, relating to the Messiah"; and Ellicott notes the form "have ye never read?" was Christ's frequent reproach to interpreters who "spent their lives in the study of the Law, and yet perverted its meaning."

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the Psalm's wonder. David means that "though every tongue were silent, God needs no other orators to proclaim his power than mere infants... still hanging on their mothers' breasts." In themselves they are silent, "but the wonderful providence of God, which shines in them, serves the purpose of splendid and powerful eloquence" — for "he who considers... how the child is formed in the mother's womb" must be "carried away into admiration of Him." As the dumb sun and moon are said to have "a loud and distinct voice for singing the praises of God" (Psalm 19:1), so Christ infers it is no strange thing that he should make the children sing his praise. Henry's tradition adds that "praise is perfected out of the mouth of such," God ordaining "strength out of the mouths of babes and sucklings," his strength "perfected in weakness."
17:
"And he left them, and went out of the city into Bethany; and he lodged there." The witnesses read the withdrawal as prudence, as poverty, and as a parable of where Christ chooses to dwell.

Pseudo-Chrysostom finds in it a rule of wisdom: "a bad man is better overcome by giving way to him than by replying to him; for wickedness is not instructed but stimulated by reproof. The Lord accordingly sought by withdrawing Himself to check those whom His words could not check." Henry's tradition agrees he left the priests "as incorrigible," departing "in prudence, lest they should now have seized him before his hour was come; in justice, because they had forfeited the favour of his presence" — "by repining at Christ's praises we drive him from us."

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the daily pattern. Clarke observes that "from this day till his death... he spent not one night in Jerusalem, but went every evening to Bethany, and returned to the city each morning" (so Luke 21:37), partly "to avoid the snares" and partly "to take away all suspicion of his affecting the regal power." Bethany lay "about two miles distant from Jerusalem, by Mount Olivet" (John 11:18). Robertson and Ellicott add that, though the name means "house of depression or misery," the home of "Martha and Mary and Lazarus" was for Jesus "a house of solace and comfort... during this week of destiny" — and they note the suppression of those friends' names in the first three Gospels, "every way significant."

Jerome draws out the poverty: Christ "was in so great poverty... that He had found in all that city neither entertainer, nor abode, but He made His home in a little village, in the house of Lazarus and his sisters." Origen turns it allegorical: Bethany is "the house of obedience," that is, the Church, where Christ "had taken rest" — "for every man who is obedient to the word of God is Bethany, and Christ abides in him; but the wicked and the sinners He leaves." So the King, having purged and healed and silenced the proud, withdraws from the unbelieving city to lodge with the obedient — the same fathers reading in the morning's barren fig-tree, which follows, the synagogue He found full of leaves and void of fruit.
18:
"Now in the morning as he returned into the city, he hungered." The witnesses dwell on Christ's faithful return to a hostile city and on the reality of his human hunger.

Henry's tradition marks the return: some think he had lodged outside "because none of his friends there durst entertain him, for fear of the great men; yet, having work to do there, he returned" — "we must never be driven off from our duty either by the malice of our foes, or the unkindness of our friends," for "in this city bonds and afflictions did abide him" (as Paul went "bound in the spirit to Jerusalem," Acts 20:22).

That "he hungered" the witnesses take as plain proof of his manhood. Clarke and Ellicott suppose "neither he, nor his disciples, had any thing but what they got from public charity," and Robertson that "Jesus spent the night out of doors and so had no breakfast"; Ellicott infers from Luke that "the greater part of the night had been spent... in solitary prayer," which explains "the exhaustion which sought food wherever there might seem even a chance of finding it." Henry adds that "the zeal of God's house did even eat him up, and his meat and drink was to do his Father's will."

Calvin, for the Reformed, faces the difficulty squarely. Christ "did not pretend hunger, but actually" felt it, "for we know that he voluntarily became subject to the infirmities of the flesh." How then was he "mistaken in seeking fruit on a tree that had none"? Calvin answers that "as man, he did not know at a distance the kind of tree," though "it is possible that he approached it on purpose, with full knowledge of the result." It was "not the fury of passion that led him to curse the tree — for that would not only have been an unjust, but even a childish and ridiculous revenge"; rather, "as hunger was troublesome to him according to the feeling of the flesh, he determined to overcome it by an opposite affection," the desire "to promote the glory of the Father" (cf. John 4:34). So his very hunger "gave him an opportunity of performing a miracle and of teaching his disciples," and of presenting "an outward sign of the end which awaits hypocrites."
19:
"And when he saw a fig tree in the way, he came to it, and found nothing thereon, but leaves only... Let no fruit grow on thee henceforward for ever. And presently the fig tree withered away." The witnesses treat the tree's ownership, the famous difficulty of the season, the meaning of the curse, and its symbolism.

The grammatical-historical commentators first clear Christ of wrong. The tree stood "in the way," by the roadside — "common property," with "no owner," so that "no injury was done to any person" (Gill, Barnes, Clarke); Lightfoot classes it among wild figs of "common right." They then meet Mark's note that "the time of figs was not yet" (Mark 11:13). Ellicott explains that the fig "bears two or three crops a year," and that this tree, "in full foliage" and "so far in advance of its fellows," might fairly be expected to have "the first ripe fruit" (Hosea 9:10; cf. Song of Solomon 2:13); Robertson adds that "the early figs start in spring before the leaves," so "there should have been figs on the tree with the crop of leaves." The leaves, in short, made a promise the tree could not keep.

On the "curse" Barnes is careful: the word "does not imply here anger, or disappointment, or malice," but only "devoting to destruction" (so the Jews used it, Hebrews 6:8). Calvin agrees it means "the tree should be condemned to barrenness," as God's voice elsewhere "bestows fertility"; and from Mark both note the withering, though "immediate" (parachrema, "on the spot," Robertson), was observed only the next day.

Most read the tree as a sign. Henry's tradition makes it represent "hypocrites in general" — "Christ looks for the power of religion from those that make profession of it" but finds "leaves only" — and "the nation and people of the Jews in particular," who "called Abraham their father, but did not do the works of Abraham." Ellicott sees the parable of Luke 13:6 "embodied," the doom now falling that had then been delayed. Barnes alone is cautious: though "a good illustration of the destruction of the Jewish people," "there is not the least evidence that our Saviour intended it as such."
20:
"And when the disciples saw it, they marvelled, saying, How soon is the fig tree withered away!" The witnesses settle the chronology and draw the lesson of the sudden judgment.

The grammatical-historical commentators align the accounts. Gill, Barnes, Ellicott, and Wesley note (from Mark 11:20) that the disciples saw the withered tree "the next day in the morning, as they returned from Bethany," and that it was Peter who, "as the spokesman of the rest, drew his Master's attention to the fact" — Matthew "means only to say that this was said to him," while Mark "tells us which one... said it." Whether the withering was seen at the moment or "inferred from its completeness when seen," Ellicott leaves open; either way the disciples marvelled chiefly at "the suddenness of the thing."

Henry's tradition presses the manner of it: there was "no visible cause of the fig-tree's withering, but it was a secret blast, a worm at the root"; "it was not only the leaves of it that withered, but the body of the tree," which "withered away in an instant and became like a dry stick." Hence, he says, "Gospel curses are... the most dreadful — that they work insensibly and silently, by a fire not blown, but effectually." The hypocrite's profession, like the tree, may stand green for a time, but, having "no root in themselves," withers from within once the curse has fallen.

Clarke turns the marvel into a warning for the living: "we often say to our neighbours, 'How suddenly this man died! Who could have expected it so soon?' But who takes warning by these examples? What we say to-day of others, may be said to-morrow of ourselves." The fit response, he urges, is the disciples' own — "Be ye also ready! Lord, increase our faith!" — which leads directly into the teaching on faith and prayer that follows. The witnesses thus hold together two notes the verse strikes at once: wonder at a power that needs no visible instrument, and sober self-examination, lest the barren tree prove a portrait of the marvelling onlooker as much as of the nation under judgement.
21:
"If ye have faith, and doubt not... ye shall say unto this mountain, Be thou removed, and be thou cast into the sea; it shall be done." The witnesses agree the saying is proverbial, and dwell on the faith it describes.

The grammatical-historical commentators set it against rabbinic idiom. To "remove mountains" was "a proverbial form of speech" for "overcoming great difficulties" (Clarke, Barnes, Ellicott); Lightfoot supplies the very usage — the learned rabbis were styled "rooters up of mountains," as "Rabh Joseph is Sinai, and Rabbah is a rooter up of mountains," praised for "a piercing judgment." So Christ "deservedly useth" the phrase "to set forth the power of faith." Ellicott notes the form "excludes a literal fulfilment" (as in 1 Corinthians 13:2), Christ pointing to "this mountain" — the Mount of Olives — "to give greater vividness to an illustration which the disciples would readily understand" (cf. Matthew 17:20). Robertson reads "doubt not" (mê diakrithête) as "to waver, to doubt, the opposite of faith," and observes that many take the blighted tree itself as "an emblem of the Jewish people, with a great show of religion and no fruit." Barnes allows that, had they "the faith of miracles," the literal removal would have been "as easy for God... as to heal the sick," but thinks Christ "rather referred... to the difficulties and trials" of their coming ministry.

Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the verse a definition of faith. "Faith, if we have any, breaks out immediately into prayer, and penetrates into the treasures of the grace of God." Christ joins faith and prayer because, had he only promised "we shall have whatever we wish, some would have thought" it "presumptuous." Thus the passage shows "the power and nature" of faith — "a certainty, relying on the goodness of God, which does not admit of doubt"; Christ "does not acknowledge as believers any but those who are fully convinced that God is reconciled to them." Against the doubt the "Papists" mingle with faith, Calvin urges Paul's "boldness to approach to God with confidence" (Ephesians 3:12). Yet he guards it: Christ "does not give a loose rein to the wishes of men," but "places prayers after the rule of faith," so that the Spirit holds "all our affections by the bridle of the word of God."
22:
"And all things, whatsoever ye shall ask in prayer, believing, ye shall receive." The witnesses read this as the chapter's closing promise, weighing its breadth, its condition, and its first recipients.

The point of the saying, says Robertson, is "faith in the efficacy of prayer" — the same lesson "of the parable of the mountain." Gill takes the "all things" broadly: "not only miracles, but any other thing which may be for the honour of God, the interest of religion, the spreading of the Gospel... their own spiritual good, and the welfare of immortal souls." Clarke fits it into the order of salvation — "a conviction of the want of it" begets "prayer, or warm desires," which the soul puts into words, "believes the word of promise," and so "receives, according to the merciful promise of God, the salvation which his soul requires."

Ellicott supplies the necessary qualification. As in Matthew 7:7, there is "the implied condition... that what is asked is in harmony with the laws and will of God"; for "if it were not so it would not be asked in faith, and every true prayer involves the submission of what it asks to the divine judgment." He adds a striking christological note: the promise suggests, as is fully expressed in John 11:42, that "our Lord's miracles were less frequently wrought by an inherent supernatural virtue — though this also distinctly appears" (as with the woman with the issue of blood, Luke 8:46) — "than by power received from the Father, and in answer to His own prayers."

Barnes, finally, presses a caution against over-claiming. The promise "was evidently a special one, given to them in regard to working miracles. To them it was true. But it is manifest that we have no right to apply this promise to ourselves" without limit, "nor... to turn it from its original meaning." Set beside Gill's wide application and Ellicott's qualifying clause, the witnesses together hold the promise open and yet bounded: prayer offered in real faith, and within the will of God, is never denied — the believing expectation, as Henry's tradition put it, "shall not be frustrated."
23:
"By what authority doest thou these things? and who gave thee this authority?" The witnesses examine the questioners, the double thrust of their challenge, and the wisdom of Christ's reply.

The challengers were "the chief priests and the elders" — with the scribes (Luke), "representatives of the three constituent elements of the Sanhedrin" (Ellicott); Henry distinguishes the chief priests of "the ecclesiastical court" and the elders of "the civil" (2 Chronicles 19:8), now joined to attack him. Pseudo-Chrysostom traces their motive to jealousy "because they had seen Christ entering the Temple in great glory"; Chrysostom notes that, unable "to detract from His miracles," they "bring matter of blame from His forbidding to sell in the Temple."

The two questions, the fathers observe, are distinct barbs. By "by what authority?" they renew the old slander (Matthew 12:24) "that the things He does are of the Devil"; by "who gave thee this authority?" they "most clearly deny the Son of God," supposing he works "not by His own, but by others' strength" (Jerome). Pseudo-Chrysostom hears in the second the assumption "that there be many persons who give power to men": "Thou art not come of a priestly family; the Senate has not conferred on Thee this power, neither has Caesar" — "for every man judges of others by himself," and "he who is not a Priest of God, thinks no man's Priesthood to be of God." Ellicott fills in the rabbinic background: the right to teach was conferred by the scribes on one who had studied "at the feet" of a master, marked by "the delivery of a key"; their question implied they knew "the Prophet of Nazareth had not been so admitted."

Calvin, for the Reformed, grants the question a "plausible" surface — for "a man ought not, of his own accord, to intermeddle with... the prophetical office, but ought to wait for the calling of God" (Psalm 110:4; Hebrews 7:21) — yet condemns it as malicious, since "the divine majesty of Christ had been attested by so many miracles." Origen adds a subtler reading: knowing "particular powers" are many, the priests wished to learn "the special degree" of the power in Christ, who "wrought all in one power... received of the Father"; "but because they were not worthy to hear such mysteries," he answers with a question. So, as Henry says, "those that take upon them to act with authority" should ask themselves "Who gave us that authority?" — but these asked it only "to ensnare and entangle him."
24:
"And Jesus answered and said unto them, I also will ask you one thing, which if ye tell me, I in like wise will tell you by what authority I do these things." The witnesses read the counter-question as wisdom, not evasion.

Christ "was under no obligation to give them an answer," says Barnes, for "they well knew by what authority he did this": he had openly worked miracles and claimed to be Messiah. Clarke agrees he had "already given them such proofs of his Divine mission as could not possibly be exceeded," so that further profession "would have been in vain" before men "obstinately prejudiced." Rather than refuse outright, he "took the wise in their own craftiness" — Henry calling it the prudence of those who, "as sheep in the midst of wolves," must be "wise as serpents," giving "a reason of the hope that is in us... with prudent caution, lest truth be damaged, or ourselves endangered."

Robertson notes the Greek is "one word" (logon ena): "the answer to Christ's word will give the answer to their query," for "the only human ecclesiastical authority that Jesus had came from John." Ellicott presses the logic of precedent: as one who taught "having authority, and not as the scribes" (Matthew 7:29), Christ "challenges their right to interrogate Him." Had they exercised that right with the Baptist? "If they had left his claim unquestioned, or if they had shrunk from confessing the result of their inquiry, they had virtually abdicated their office, and had no right, in logical consistency," to interrogate another teacher.

Wesley draws the connection plainly: "the baptism, that is, the whole ministry of John, was it from heaven or from men? By what authority did he act and teach? Did man or God give him that authority? Was it not God? But if so, the consequence was clear. For John testified that Jesus was the Christ." The counter-question, then, is no clever dodge but a question that already contains, for any honest mind, the answer to their own — since John, sent from heaven, had pointed to Jesus as the one whose authority they now pretended to doubt.
25:
"The baptism of John, whence was it? from heaven, or of men? And they reasoned with themselves, saying, If we shall say, From heaven; he will say unto us, Why did ye not believe him?" The witnesses unfold the dilemma and the bad faith it exposed.

"The baptism of John," all agree, stands for "his whole ministry, preaching as well as baptizing" (Henry, Gill, Barnes, Calvin). Christ's question, says Calvin, was meant "to convict them, by their own reply, of having impudently pretended ignorance of a matter with which they were well acquainted." For John "had been sent as Christ's herald" (Malachi 3:1; Luke 7:27), claiming only "to prepare the way of the Lord," and had "pointed out Christ with the finger." Hence "it was impossible to acknowledge that John was a servant of God, without acknowledging that he was Himself the Lord." The first horn was therefore fatal: confess John "from heaven," and the reply is ready — "Why did ye not believe him?"

Robertson catches "the picturesque imperfect tense" (dielogizonto) "describing their hopeless quandary." Ellicott shows the self-communing was "eminently characteristic": the priests "had halted between two opinions" — once they "came to his baptism" (Matthew 3:7), once they said "He hath a devil" (Matthew 11:18) — watching "the ebb and flow of a public reverence which the death of John had deepened," "reluctant to admit" his prophetic character "because this would have involved the necessity of accepting the testimony which he had borne to... Jesus."

Calvin draws from the verse a standing principle: Christ "intended to draw out a reply, Was John a true and lawful prophet of God, or an impostor?" — teaching that "no doctrine and no sacrament ought to be received among the godly, unless it be evident that it has come from God." "The whole government of the Church depends on the will of God in such a manner, that men have no right to introduce any thing from themselves" — a thrust against "the pretended sacraments, which men of no authority have foolishly introduced." The priests, however, "do not inquire what is true," but "choose rather to shuffle than to acknowledge what they know to be true, that their tyranny may not be impaired."
26:
"But if we shall say, Of men; we fear the people; for all hold John as a prophet." The witnesses dwell on the second horn of the dilemma and on what the rulers' fear reveals.

Barnes states the danger plainly: "they feared that the people would stone them" (so Luke), for "such an unpopular sentiment as to profess that all that John did was imposture would have probably ended in tumult, perhaps in their death" — "all held John as a prophet" (Matthew 14:5). Gill adds that to call John's ministry "merely human... what he took up of himself" was the only alternative to confessing it divine, and it was an alternative they dared not speak aloud.

Henry's tradition draws out what the confession lays bare. First, "the people had truer sentiments of John than the chief priests and the elders had": the common people, despised by the rulers as those who "knew not the law, and were cursed," in fact "knew the gospel, and were blessed." Second, that "the chief priests and elders stood in awe of the common people" is "an evidence that things were in disorder among them," the government "become obnoxious to the hatred and scorn of the people," fulfilling the word, "I have made you contemptible and base" (Malachi 2:9). "Those could not but fear the people, who studied only how to make the people fear them."

Most pointedly, Henry observes that "the chief priests and elders were kept from an open denial of the truth, even against the conviction of their own minds, not by the fear of God, but purely by the fear of the people." So "the fear of man" — which "may bring good people into a snare" (Proverbs 29:25) — here serves only to keep "bad people from being overmuch wicked": "many bad people would be much worse than they are, if they durst." Their calculation weighed not truth against falsehood but credit against safety, the constant mark, the witnesses note, of those who "seek their own things." The same fear, Henry adds, that once kept ordinary believers from confessing Christ openly now grips the rulers themselves — a reversal in which the shepherds tremble before the flock they ought to have led.
27:
"And they answered Jesus, and said, We cannot tell. And he said unto them, Neither tell I you by what authority I do these things." The witnesses read the rulers' reply as a deliberate lie and a self-condemning abdication.

"We cannot tell," Barnes insists, "was a direct falsehood. They could have told; and it should have been, we will not tell." Clarke makes the same correction: "in the words of truth" it "should have been, We will not tell, for we will not have this man for the Messiah; because, if we acknowledge John as his forerunner, we must, of necessity, receive Jesus as the Christ." So the men who "at other times took upon them to judge of a prophet, whether he was true or false," now, "contrary to their office and character, declare they did not know" (Gill). Henry's tradition notes the irony that "when they would not confess their knowledge, they were constrained to confess their ignorance" — and that "there are many who are more afraid of the shame of lying than of the sin."

Ellicott calls "the confession of impotence... a virtual abdication." "Before such a tribunal the Prophet whom they called in question might well refuse to plead. There was, indeed, no need to answer. For those who were not wilfully blind and deaf, the words that He had spoken, the works which He had done, the sinless life which He had led, were proofs of an authority from God."

Christ's refusal is therefore just, not evasive. Those "obstinately prejudiced against the strongest conviction" are not "fit to be discoursed with concerning Christ's authority"; to argue further would be "to cast pearls before swine." Henry's tradition grounds it in a sober principle: "those that imprison the truths they know in unrighteousness" — by neither professing nor practising them — "are justly denied the further truths they enquire after" (Romans 1:18). Clarke adds the warning that God "often hides from the wise and prudent what he reveals unto babes," so that men who "use their wisdom only to invent... excuses for rejecting the truth" are "punished with that ignorance to which, in their own defence, they are obliged to have recourse."
28:
"But what think ye? A certain man had two sons; and he came to the first, and said, Son, go work to day in my vineyard." The witnesses read the parable's purpose, its imagery, and its disputed cast of characters.

Its aim, says Henry's tradition, is to convict by self-judgment: like Nathan before David (2 Samuel 12:1), "reproving parables are appeals to the offenders themselves, and judge them out of their own mouths." Pseudo-Chrysostom marvels at the boldness — "those who are to be judged in this cause, He applies to as judges" — and Christ "veils the allusion... that they might not perceive that they were passing sentence upon themselves."

The imagery the witnesses read together. The father "is... God, who created all men," "a common Father to all mankind" (Pseudo-Chrysostom, Henry); the vineyard is "the work which God requires man to do" (Barnes), "vineyard work, creditable, profitable, and pleasant" (Henry); and "to day" means "during this age," requiring "present obedience" — "the night comes when no man can work."

On the two sons the readings diverge. Jerome and Pseudo-Chrysostom take the first (who refuses, then repents) as the Gentiles and the second (who promises, then fails) as the Jews who said at Sinai, "All that the Lord hath said... we will do" (Exodus 24:3). Others, Jerome notes, read it simply of "the righteous and... sinners" — the publicans who repented under John against the Pharisees who only "made a shew of righteousness." Origen frames it generally: against "those who promise great things but do none of these things," yet adds the Jews "are not shut out," for "when the fulness of the Gentiles shall have entered in, then all Israel shall be saved" (Romans 11:25). Pseudo-Chrysostom himself finds the Gentile/Jew scheme strained, since "publicans and harlots" are Jews, and settles on a reading of "the common sort... better than the Priests," the penitent people against the impenitent rulers. Calvin gathers the point: Christ "unmasks those hypocrites, that they may no longer boast of being the ministers of God." A textual note runs through the comments: Jerome insists "the correct copies" read "the first," "that they might be condemned by their own sentence," though some manuscripts reverse the order.
29:
"He answered and said, I will not: but afterward he repented, and went." The witnesses dwell on the blunt refusal, the change of mind, and the precise word for it.

The "I will not," says Ellicott, matches "the rough recklessness of the classes (publicans and harlots)" figured by the first son, "whose whole life, up to the time of their conversion, had been an open refusal to keep God's laws." Clarke calls it "the general reply of every sinner to the invitations of God," in which "the Most High is treated without ceremony or respect." Gill, for the Reformed, sees in it a portrait of the natural will: "where is man's free will? this is the true picture of it; man has no will naturally to that which is good" — until grace turns him.

The turn comes in "afterward he repented." Here the grammatical commentators are careful. The word, Robertson stresses, is metamelêtheis, which "really means 'repent,' to be sorry afterwards," but "must be sharply distinguished from" metanoeô (Matthew 3:2) and metanoia (Matthew 3:8); it denotes mere "sorrow," which may pass into true repentance or end in remorse. Ellicott agrees it "expresses rather the regretful change of purpose than entire transformation of character" — "the first stage of repentance," which may rise higher or, as with Judas (Matthew 27:3, the same word), "end only in remorse and despair." In this son, however, the sorrow bore fruit: "the boy got sorry for his stubborn refusal... and went and obeyed." Paul's rule applies — "godly sorrow leads to repentance" (cf. 2 Corinthians 7:9), and here it did.

The witnesses thus find in the first son the pattern of grace toward the openly rebellious. As Henry's tradition put it, "there are many who in the beginning are wicked and wilful, and very unpromising, who afterward repent and mend": "such were some of you" (1 Corinthians 6:11). The only proof of repentance for past resistance, the witnesses agree, is to "comply, and set to work" — "better late than never." The son who said No and went, they conclude, did the father's will more truly than fair words ever could, since obedience is measured not by the promise of the lips but by the labour of the hands.
30:
"And he came to the second, and said likewise. And he answered and said, I go, sir: and went not." The witnesses make this son the very image of the hypocrite who promises but does not perform.

The "I go, sir" the commentators read as outward respect without substance. Ellicott calls "the tone of outward respect, as contrasted with the rude refusal of the elder son... eminently characteristic as representing the surface religion of the Pharisees." Calvin notes the phrase "is borrowed from the Hebrew language; for, when the Hebrews wish to offer their services... they speak in this manner, 'Here I am, Sir.'" Prompt obedience is "a laudable virtue in itself," and "Christ does not here give the commendation to slowness"; but since "both are improper — to delay before doing your duty, and to promise what you do not perform — Christ shows that this hypocrisy is less to be endured than the fierceness which, in process of time, is subdued."

The failure lies wholly in the "went not." Clarke laments "what a multitude of such are in the world, professing to know God, but denying him in their works! Alas! what will such professions avail, when God comes to take away the soul?" Wesley applies it directly: "just so did the scribes and Pharisees: they professed the greatest readiness and zeal in the service of God; but it was bare profession, contradicted by all their actions."

Henry's tradition deepens the warning: "there are many that give good words, and make fair promises in religion... that rest there, and go no further, and so come to nothing. Saying and doing are two things" — the very fault "charged upon the Pharisees" (Matthew 23:3). "Many with their mouth show much love, but their heart goes another way"; meeting "something to be done that was too hard, or something to be parted with that was too dear," their good purposes prove "to no purpose." "Buds and blossoms are not fruit." So the second son, fair in word and barren in deed, stands as the more dangerous of the two — the formalist whose surface devotion, the witnesses agree, more easily hardens than the open rebel's refusal.
31:
"Whether of them twain did the will of his father? They say unto him, The first. Jesus saith unto them, Verily I say unto you, That the publicans and the harlots go into the kingdom of God before you." The witnesses fasten on the self-condemning verdict and the startling reversal it draws.

The answer, Barnes notes, "was correct. But it is strange that they did not perceive that it condemned themselves." Ellicott presses the irony: the verdict "came apparently from the lips of the very persons who were self-condemned by it," in "something like an unconsciousness that they were described in the person of the second son." "They who gave God thanks that they were not like other men could not imagine for an instant that the 'went not' represented their spiritual life." The first son was preferred, Henry's tradition explains, "because his actions were better than his words, and his latter end than his beginning" — agreeing with God's own rule of judgment, that "if the sinner turn from his wickedness, he shall be pardoned" (Ezekiel 18:21).

Then comes the reversal. "Go into the kingdom of God before you," says Robertson, means "the publicans and harlots march ahead of the ecclesiastics into the kingdom" — "a powerful indictment of the complacency of the Jewish theological leaders." Ellicott notes the word "literally means, lead the way into," and is "significant as implying that there was still time for scribes and Pharisees to follow in the rear": "the door was not yet closed against them, though those whom they despised had taken the place of honour."

The "publicans and the harlots" are named, Ellicott observes, "purposely general, as describing the action of classes," yet "we cannot help associating them with the personal instances" — the publican who became an Apostle (Matthew 9:9), Zacchaeus (Luke 19:2), and "the woman that was a sinner" (Luke 7:37). Clarke draws the contrast: "in all their former conduct they had said No," but now "yield to the voice of truth," while the others, "always professing the most ready... obedience," "did not receive it when it came."
32:
"For John came unto you in the way of righteousness, and ye believed him not: but the publicans and the harlots believed him: and ye, when ye had seen it, repented not afterward, that ye might believe him." The witnesses unfold John's vindication and the rulers' aggravated unbelief.

"In the way of righteousness" the commentators read two ways. Ellicott takes it "in a half-technical sense, as expressing the aspect of righteousness which the Pharisees themselves recognised" (Matthew 6:1) — the almsgiving, fasting, and prayer "conspicuous both in the life and in the teaching of the Baptist." Clarke prefers "John came unto you, who are in the way of righteousness," the Jews being "in the way" of revelation as the Gentiles were not. Henry's tradition uses it as proof "that John's baptism was from heaven": "by their fruits ye shall know them"; John "taught people to repent, and to work the works of righteousness," and was himself "a great example of strictness."

The success of his ministry seals it. The "publicans and the harlots believed him" — the former named among his hearers (Luke 3:12), the latter feeling "the impulse of the strong popular movement" (cf. Luke 7:37). "If God had not sent John," Henry's tradition argues, "he would not have crowned his labours with such wonderful success." Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the believing more than assent: "faith does not consist solely in a person's giving his assent to true doctrine, but... the hearer, renouncing himself, devotes his life wholly to God."

The sharpest word is the last. "Repented not afterward" means, better, "did not even repent afterward" (Ellicott); the rulers were "like the first of the two sons in his defiant refusal," but "not like him in his subsequent repentance." They were not even "provoked to a holy emulation" (Romans 11:14) by the sight of publicans and harlots going before them. Calvin calls it "an aggravated view of their malice," "a mark of people altogether depraved... not to follow, at the very least, when the publicans and the harlots point out... the road." Wesley closes with a sigh: "O how is this scripture fulfilled at this day!"
33:
"Hear another parable: There was a certain householder, which planted a vineyard, and hedged it round about, and digged a winepress in it, and built a tower, and let it out to husbandmen, and went into a far country." The witnesses ground the parable in Isaiah, expound its imagery, and read its design.

All trace its frame to Isaiah 5:1–7, where "the vineyard of the Lord of hosts is the house of Israel" (Jerome, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson, Calvin); Origen marks the difference — in Isaiah "the threats... are against the vineyard," but "in the Gospel... the husbandmen are blamed." The elements the commentators read with one broad voice, though with variant detail. The householder is God, "who in some parables is represented as a man" (Origen) — "Father" by goodness, "Lord" by nature (Pseudo-Chrysostom). The vineyard is Israel, or "the church... furnished with all things requisite" (Henry). The hedge is "the institutions which made Israel a separate... people" (Ellicott), or "the guardianship of Angels" (Jerome), or God's "special protection" (Henry). The winepress (better the wine-vat, Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott) is "the altar of burnt-offerings" (Henry) or the Temple "into which the wine of devotion... was to flow" (Ellicott). The tower is "the Temple" (Jerome), "Jerusalem and the outward polity" (Ellicott), or "the eminence of the Law... from which... the coming of Christ might be spied" (Hilary). The husbandmen are "the chief priests and elders" (Henry), and the "far country" not a change of place — "God, by whom all things are filled, cannot be absent" (Jerome) — but his leaving "the vine-dressers a freedom of acting."

On the design John Chrysostom is fullest: the parable shows "God's providence... exercised towards them from the first," "their murderous disposition from the beginning," that "the God both of the New and of the Old Testament was one and the same," and finally "the calling of the Gentiles, the casting out of the Jews." Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the stewardship: as God complained by Isaiah and Jeremiah (Jeremiah 2:21) that he "received no fruit," so here "he accuses the vine-dressers themselves, who, like base swindlers, appropriate to themselves the produce." Barnes alone urges restraint: the circumstances "serve to keep up the story," but "where he has not explained them, we have no right" to press a meaning on each.
34:
"And when the time of the fruit drew near, he sent his servants to the husbandmen, that they might receive the fruits of it." The witnesses identify the servants and the rent expected of the tenants.

With one voice they read the servants as the prophets and the husbandmen as Israel's leaders. The "servants" are God's "extraordinary messengers, the prophets," sent "to the husbandmen," the "ordinary preachers or ministers of the Jews" (Wesley); they "represent the prophets sent to the Jewish people" (Barnes), whom God "sent... from time to time to call both priests and people back to the purity of his holy religion" (Clarke). Robertson notes these "slaves are distinguished from the husbandmen" — the workers who had leased the vineyard — and that "the conduct of the husbandmen towards the householder's slaves portrays the behaviour of the Jewish people and the religious leaders... towards the prophets and now towards Christ."

The rent itself the commentators explain from custom. The "time of the fruit" was the season of gathering, when "the owner sent to receive the part that was his" (Barnes), for "the vineyard was let out... for a part of the fruit." Clarke notes "the ancient custom of paying the rent of a farm in kind," still found in his day "in the highlands of Scotland." Gill recalls the law that the fruit of trees was not to be eaten for the first years and was holy in the fourth (cf. Leviticus 19:23–25).

Ellicott, however, cautions against pressing the "successive sendings" too closely; "we cannot find any exact parallel in the history of Israel." It is "enough to see in them the general expectation" — echoing Isaiah's "I looked that it should bring forth grapes" (Isaiah 5:4) — "that the developed life of Israel should be worthy of its calling, and the mission of the prophets who... were sent from time to time to call the people to bring forth the fruits of righteousness." The fruit God seeks, the witnesses agree, is not produce but the obedience of a people, rendered through the rulers entrusted with them.
35:
"And the husbandmen took his servants, and beat one, and killed another, and stoned another." The witnesses hear in the three verbs the long history of the persecution of the prophets.

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the violence to its instances. "Beat one," says Barnes, "properly means to flay, or to take off the skin" — hence "to whip, so that the skin in many places is taken off"; Clarke and Lightfoot agree it pictures excessive scourging. "Killed another" recalls how "Isaiah is said to have been put to death by sawing him asunder," and "stoned another" the common Jewish penalty, as Zechariah son of Jehoiada was "stoned... in the court of the house of the Lord" (2 Chronicles 24:21). Lightfoot connects the verbs with "the four kinds of death... delivered to the Sanhedrim — stoning, burning, killing, and strangling." Barnes and Ellicott gather the wider testimony — that the prophets "as a class, had no light or easy task" but were "called upon, one by one, to suffer persecution" (Hebrews 11:37; 2 Chronicles 36:16; Nehemiah 9:26) — the very charge Christ presses in Matthew 23:37.

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out God's persistence against their malice. Where Mark and Luke name the servants "one at a time," Matthew "explains the matter more at large, namely, that God, by sending a multitude of prophets, contended with the malice of the priests." "God did not, on account of the cruelty of the priests, fail to send prophets; but raising them up — as it were — in troops, fought against their malice." Hence, Calvin observes, "how obstinate their malice was, for the correction of which no remedies were of any avail." The husbandmen, the witnesses agree, returned not only no fruit but violence for the very mercy that sought it — a portrait, as Henry's tradition put it, of "what hath all along been the lot of God's faithful messengers." And the lesson reaches beyond Israel: "if they that live godly in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution, much more they that press others to it" — the rulers, set to gather fruit for God, became instead the fiercest enemies of those he sent.
36:
"Again, he sent other servants more than the first: and they did unto them likewise." The witnesses dwell on the meaning of "more than the first" and on the persistence of grace and of sin alike.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the phrase. Clarke argues that the Greek (pleionas) should be rendered "more honourable," not "more than the first" — for "as the fullness of the time approached, each prophet more clearly and fully pointed out the coming of Christ," whereas "more than the first... conveys no meaning at all." Ellicott reads "a reference here to the greater power and fulness of the work of the later prophets, such as Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel, stretching onward to that of the Baptist, as closing the whole line." Gill suggests the second sending may include "such as suffered in the times of the Maccabees."

Clarke draws the broader lesson that God never ceases to send: "there is not a moment in which God does not shower down his gifts upon men, and require the fruit of them.
