Various instruments are used to bring sinners to God — prophets, apostles, pastors, teachers." And he notes the unhappy constancy of the result: "the true disciples of Christ have been persecuted in all ages, and the greatest share of the persecution has fallen upon the ministers of his religion; for there have always been good and bad husbandmen, and the latter have persecuted the former."

The verse thus sets two persistencies side by side. Henry's tradition marvels at "the riches of the patience and forbearance of God, in keeping up in his church a despised, persecuted ministry"; he "sent other servants, more than the first," though "the first sped not." Yet the husbandmen "persisted in their wickedness," for "one sin makes way for another of the same kind." The escalating mercy of God and the hardening malice of men, the witnesses agree, run on together until the sending of the Son. Each fresh embassy heightened both the privilege despised and the guilt incurred, so that the second band, sent in greater fulness, met no better welcome than the first — proof, the commentators note, that the fault lay not in any deficiency of means but wholly in the will of the husbandmen.
37:
"But last of all he sent unto them his son, saying, They will reverence my son." The witnesses dwell on the climax of grace and on the "deliberation" ascribed to God.

This sending, all agree, is the parable's summit. The other Gospels make it more tender still — "he had yet one son, his beloved" (Mark 12:6); "I will send my beloved son, it may be they will reverence him" (Luke 20:13). Ellicott sees in it "the gradually ascending scale of those who were sent, culminating in a difference not of degree only, but of kind, like the contrast between the prophets and the Son" (Hebrews 1:1–2), and "the employment by God, in His long-suffering pity, of all possible means to lead His people to repentance." Barnes makes it the very mirror of the gospel: "this beautifully and most tenderly exhibits the love of God, in sending his only Son... to die for men" (cf. John 3:16; 1 John 4:9); the Son is one "whom the world ought to reverence, even as they should the Father."

The words "they will reverence my son" raised an old question, since God cannot be deceived in his expectation. Calvin answers that "strictly speaking, this thought does not apply to God; for He knew what would happen," but "it is customary, especially in parables, to ascribe to Him human feelings." The clause was added "not without reason," to "represent, as in a mirror, how deplorable their impiety was" — that "they rose in diabolical rage against the Son of God, who had come to bring them back to a sound mind." Clarke notes the verb means "they will reflect... and blush for shame because of it, when they see my son." So the sending of the Son both crowns the householder's patience and lays bare, beyond all excuse, the wickedness that would reject even him. Henry's tradition presses the point: the Son "comes with more authority than the servants could," for "judgment is committed to him, that all men should honour him"; reverence paid to him would have been "a powerful and effectual principle of fruitfulness and obedience," and there is "greater danger in refusing him than in despising Moses' law." That the householder reserved his Son for last shows this was the final remedy, beyond which no further means remained.
38:
"But when the husbandmen saw the son, they said among themselves, This is the heir; come, let us kill him, and let us seize on his inheritance." The witnesses ask how far the rulers acted in knowledge, and what the "inheritance" meant.

Several insist the rulers knew whom they killed. Lightfoot compares the verse with John 11:48 and concludes "the rulers of the Jews acknowledged among themselves that Christ was the Messias," yet "put him to death, lest, bringing in another worship and another people, he should... suppress their worship and themselves." Ellicott points to Caiaphas's counsel "that one man should die for the people" (John 11:49), and to those "who believed on Him, and yet, through fear of the Pharisees, were not confessed disciples" (John 12:42): the parable "showed that they stood face to face with One who knew the secrets of their hearts." Gill allows that "some were entirely ignorant," but that these men "acted against light and conscience, with spite and malice."

The "inheritance" they coveted the witnesses read as their power over the people. Robertson renders the plot "let us get his inheritance" (an ingressive, hortatory subjunctive); Barnes notes "it was rented to them, was in their possession, and they resolved to keep it." Calvin's reading (from the previous verse) supplies the motive: "the chief reason why the priests raged against Christ was, that they might not lose their tyranny," for he is the one "by whom God chooses to govern."

Barnes cautions that the detail "has probably no reference to any particular conduct of the Jews, but is thrown in to keep up the story." Yet the witnesses agree the plot mirrors the historical conspiracy (cf. Matthew 27:1), and that the Son named "heir" answers to the prophetic word that God would give him "the heathen for thine inheritance" — an inheritance, Raban added, "not left Him by His Father when dying, but wonderfully purchased by His own death" (cf. Hebrews 1:2). Paul's word that "had they known it, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory" (1 Corinthians 2:8) the witnesses reconcile with the rulers' knowing malice by distinguishing the blind instruments — Pilate and the Roman power — from the chief priests who, against light and conscience, recognised the heir and slew him to keep the vineyard their own.
39:
"And they caught him, and cast him out of the vineyard, and slew him." The witnesses read the three acts as the arrest, rejection, and crucifixion of Christ.

Gill takes "they caught him" of the arrest "in the garden, by a band of soldiers and officers... with swords and staves," who "took him, bound him, and led him away." Barnes refers the whole to "the conduct of the Jews in putting the Saviour to death" — "by giving him into the hands of the Romans, and seeking his crucifixion" (cf. Acts 2:23; Acts 7:52) — and notes that "so they understood it" (v.45).

The order of the words — "cast him out of the vineyard, and slew him" — the commentators find significant. Barnes and Ellicott see in it "a latent reference to the facts" that "our Lord was delivered over to the judgment of the Gentiles" and that "He was crucified outside the Holy City" (John 19:20; Hebrews 13:12), the vineyard standing for Jerusalem, "in a special sense... the vineyard of the Lord of Hosts." So Jesus "was crucified out of Jerusalem, on Mount Calvary" (Luke 23:33) — cast out as though "the shame and reproach" of his people, who was in truth "the greatest glory of his people Israel."

Clarke turns the verse inward, to every impenitent heart: to "cast him out of the vineyard" is to "utterly reject the counsel of God," to "neither acknowledge the authority of Christ, nor submit to his teaching" — "a sinner, to enjoy a little longer his false peace... rejects Jesus, and persecutes that Gospel which troubles his sinful repose." The husbandmen's final crime, the witnesses agree, is the murder of the Son foretold and then enacted within the week — the hinge on which the parable's judgment turns. The casting out before the slaying, Henry's tradition adds, fits the history exactly: they reckoned him "unworthy to live," thrust him "out of the holy city," and crucified him "without the gate" (Hebrews 13:12), treating as "the shame and reproach" the one who was the true glory of Israel — so that, as men dealt with the servants, they dealt at last with the Son.
40:
"When the lord therefore of the vineyard cometh, what will he do unto those husbandmen?" The witnesses read the question as Christ's device to draw the rulers into condemning themselves.

Gill takes the coming of the lord as God's coming "in a way of providence, to call these husbandmen to an account; not only for the fruit they were to bring to him, but for their barbarous treatment of his servants, the prophets... and especially, for the inhuman usage and murder of his own son." Barnes states the purpose of the question plainly: "Jesus then asked them a question about the proper way of dealing with those men. The design of asking them this question was that they might condemn themselves, and admit the justice of the punishment that was soon coming upon them."

Henry's tradition draws out both the certainty of the reckoning and the wisdom of the appeal. "Our Saviour, in his question, supposes that the lord of the vineyard will come, and reckon with them. God is the Lord of the vineyard; the property is his, and he will make them know it, who now lord it over his heritage as if it were all their own." Against the presumption of persecutors who "say in their hearts, He delays his coming," the witnesses set the warning that "though he bear long with them, he will not bear always": "it is comfort to abused saints and ministers, that the Lord is at hand, the Judge stands before the door."

The form of the question — put "to themselves, for their stronger conviction" — belongs, Henry notes, to the same method as Nathan's with David: "God's proceedings are so unexceptionable, that there needs but an appeal to sinners themselves concerning the equity of them." By asking them to pronounce the sentence, Christ ensures that "knowing the judgment of God against them which do such things, they might be the more inexcusable" — so that, whatever they answered, they would "be justified" in condemning the very wickedness that was their own. Many, Henry observes, "can easily prognosticate the dismal consequences of other people's sins, that see not what will be the end of their own" — and so these rulers, asked to judge the husbandmen, pronounced without flinching a doom they did not yet perceive to be a description of themselves.
41:
"They say unto him, He will miserably destroy those wicked men, and will let out his vineyard unto other husbandmen, which shall render him the fruits in their seasons." The witnesses note the self-condemning verdict, its wordplay, and the apparent difference among the Gospels.

The answer, Barnes observes, was given "according as they knew men would act and would act justly" — "so clear that they could not answer otherwise" — and yet "they did not... see the bearing of what they had admitted." Robertson catches the Greek's "paronomasia or assonance": kakous kakôs apolesei, "he will put the wretches to a wretched death." The destruction, Henry's tradition adds, "was fulfilled upon the Jews, in that miserable destruction which was brought upon them by the Romans... about forty years after this," and falls still on all who, having "enjoyed the greatest share of church privileges," do not improve them.

The "other husbandmen," all agree, are the Gentiles — the church to whom the kingdom passes (cf. Romans 11:11). Ellicott calls their answer, "like the speech of Caiaphas" (John 11:49–51), "an unconscious prophecy, in which were wrapt up at once the destruction of the Holy City, and the transfer of the privileges that had belonged to Israel to the Gentile Church."

A harmony question remains: Luke reports that they said "God forbid" (Luke 20:16). Augustine resolves it by supposing "of the listening multitudes some answered as Matthew relates, and some as Luke" — or that the words, "being true, might well be said to have been spoken by Him who is truth." Ellicott prefers to read in the priests "an affected horror, by which they sought to disguise the conviction that the parable was meant for them." Either way, the verdict they pronounced — destruction for the wicked, the vineyard given to the faithful — was, in the witnesses' reading, their own sentence and an oracle of the gospel's passage to the nations. God will have a church in the world, Henry's tradition concludes, "notwithstanding the unworthiness and opposition of many that abuse the privileges of it"; if one people forfeit the vineyard, "the Lord of the vineyard would not be robbed of its fruits, and sooner or later would find faithful and true labourers."
42:
"Did ye never read in the scriptures, The stone which the builders rejected, the same is become the head of the corner: this is the Lord's doing, and it is marvellous in our eyes?" The witnesses expound the citation, the figure of the stone, and the divine hand behind it.

The quotation is from Psalm 118:22–23, drawn, Ellicott notes, "in the immediate context of the verse which had supplied the 'hosanna' shouts of the multitude." Its first reference, the commentators agree, was to David — "at first rejected by the Jewish rulers, but... afterwards chosen by the Lord to be the great ruler of his people" (Clarke) — yet "no less to Jesus" (Barnes). The "builders" are "the chief priests and elders... with the doctors of the law" (Clarke), who "had the oversight of the Jewish church, which was God's building," but "would not allow Christ a place." The "stone" rejected for want of comeliness (cf. Isaiah 53:2) is Christ, whom God made "the foundation of the church" — the figure recurring throughout the New Testament (Acts 4:11; Romans 9:33; Ephesians 2:20; 1 Peter 2:7), and grounded by Isaiah's "Behold, I lay in Zion... a precious corner stone" (Isaiah 28:16). Ellicott adds that, in this context, "the stone which the builders rejected" passes into "the nation bringing forth the fruits," the corner-stone "of the great edifice of the Catholic Church," yet not incompatibly with Christ himself being that stone (cf. Ephesians 1:22).

"This is the Lord's doing" the Reformed read as the heart of the matter. Calvin says the verse proves Christ "would be placed on his throne by the wonderful power of God, contrary to the will of men": "however fiercely soever he may be assailed by those who appear to possess honor and dignity, he will nevertheless remain in his own rank." Two lessons follow — that we "may not be perplexed by the wicked attempts of men," God "having warned us beforehand," and that "in setting up the kingdom of Christ, His power will be victorious." Robertson sums the whole as "a withering indictment": "these experts in building God's temple had rejected the corner-stone chosen by God," but "God has the last word."
43:
"Therefore say I unto you, The kingdom of God shall be taken from you, and given to a nation bringing forth the fruits thereof." The witnesses read the verse as the sentence of transfer from Israel to the Gentiles.

The application is plain to all. The Jews "had been the children of the kingdom... his chosen and peculiar people," but now "this privilege should be taken away" and "the blessing... given to a nation who would bring forth the fruits thereof; that is, to the Gentiles" (Barnes, citing Acts 28:28). Clarke makes the "fruits" the returns of grace — "as in the parable an allusion is made to paying the landlord in kind, so here the Gentiles are represented as paying God thus," for "nothing can ever be acceptable in the sight of God that does not spring from himself." Robertson calls the words "the death-knell of the Jewish nation with their hopes of political and religious world leadership."

Calvin, for the Reformed, notes that Christ now turns from the rulers to "the people themselves," who "had been the companions and assistants of the priests and scribes in hindering the grace of God." The threat, "once spoken to them, was written for the sake of all of us," lest "we grow wanton through a vain and wicked confidence in the flesh." His warning is sharp: "if he spared not the natural branches" (Romans 11:21), "what will he do with those which were engrafted? ... much less is the kingdom of God bound to us, if it be not rooted in true godliness."

Yet the same verse yields comfort. "The perpetuity of that kingdom... may afford comfort to all the godly," Calvin says; "though the ungodly destroyed the worship of God among themselves, they would never cause the name of Christ to be abolished," for God "will find elsewhere a dwelling and habitation for his kingdom." The lesson, he adds, is "that the Gospel is not preached in order that it may lie barren and inoperative, but that it may yield fruit" — the very fruit the husbandmen withheld and the new nation would render in its seasons.
44:
"And whosoever shall fall on this stone shall be broken: but on whomsoever it shall fall, it will grind him to powder." The witnesses distinguish two ruins and ground the imagery in Scripture and in Jewish custom.

The two clauses, the commentators agree, describe two degrees of judgment. To "fall on this stone" is to stumble at Christ in his humiliation — those "offended at the outward lowliness of Him who came as the carpenter's son" (Ellicott), an allusion to Isaiah 8:14–15, the "stone of stumbling." Such a fall "brought with it pain and humiliation," yet "was not irretrievable" — "the bruise might be healed; it was the work of the Christ to heal it" (Ellicott); the stumbler "is broken... yet is not altogether crushed, but is preserved to salvation through endurance" (Jerome's reading). But for the stone to "fall" on a man — "a rapid transition to... the very words of Daniel 2:34–35, 44" — is final: it will "grind him to powder," the word meaning to winnow and scatter as chaff, "the destruction of all that resists Christ's kingdom" (Ellicott; cf. Matthew 16:18).

Several anchor the image in the manner of Jewish execution. Lightfoot, Barnes, and Clarke describe the stoning-scaffold "twice the height of a man," from which the culprit was cast on a stone; "if he died... well; if not, the other witness threw a [heavy] stone upon his heart." Robertson likens the shattering to "an automobile that dashes against a stone wall." The witnesses also note the textual question — the verse is "wanting in the Codex Bezae... and Origen," but stands in the parallel Luke 20:18 (Clarke, Robertson).

Calvin draws the pastoral edge. Christ "suffers no loss... when he is rejected by the wicked," but "by his own hardness he will break them," so that "he will be the more highly glorified in their destruction." The doctrine "partly instructs us to give ourselves up gently, with a mild and tractable heart, to the dominion of Christ, and partly fortifies us against the obstinacy... of the wicked, for whom there awaits a dreadful end." Gill adds that this is spoken not of believers, whose stumbling is healed, but of those to whom Christ is "a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offence."
45:
"And when the chief priests and Pharisees had heard his parables, they perceived that he spake of them." The witnesses note the moment of recognition and the response of guilty hearts.

The recognition was unmistakable. Robertson reads the verb (egnôsan) as ingressive — "there was no mistaking the meaning of these parables; the dullest could see the point." Jerome observes that "hard as were the hearts of the Jews in unbelief, they yet perceived that the Lord's sentence was directed against themselves," and Ellicott that "the real or affected unconsciousness of the drift of our Lord's teaching was at last broken through... and they were roused to a passionate desire for revenge."

Pseudo-Chrysostom draws the moral of how the guilty receive reproof: "the good man, when taken in a sin, has sorrow because he has sinned; the bad man is grieved, not because he has sinned, but because he is found out... and he not only does not repent, but is indignant with him that reproved him." So "they, being taken in their sins, were stirred up to still greater wickedness." Raban sees the scene "daily acted in the Church, when any who is a brother only in name is ashamed or afraid to assail the unity of faith and peace which he does not love, because of the good men with whom he lives."

Origen distinguishes two desires to "lay hands" on Jesus — the rulers' murderous grasp, and the Bride's longing, "I held him, and would not let him go" (Song of Solomon 3:4) — adding that "the word of truth none can seize... nor do it to death." Calvin, for the Reformed, finds the verse instructive for every age: "it is impossible but that the rage of ungodly men will be more and more inflamed by threatenings," for the word, as "a hot iron," wounds "bad consciences." Therefore "we ought... to pray that he would subdue us to voluntary fear, lest the mere knowledge of his vengeance should exasperate us the more." The recognition that ought to have driven them to repentance drove them instead to fury — the surest sign, the witnesses agree, that the word had found its mark and that their hearts, far from yielding, were hardening toward the deed the parable foretold.
46:
"But when they sought to lay hands on him, they feared the multitude, because they took him for a prophet." The chapter closes on the rulers' restrained malice and on the contrast it draws with Christ's fearlessness.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the restraint as a providential bridle. Gill notes they "did not by any outward action... apprehend him," but "secretly wished, and earnestly desired" to, "so irritated and provoked... that they could scarcely keep their hands off him." Barnes says "they would have gratified their malice at once, but they feared the people," who "took him for a prophet" (cf. Luke 7:16; John 7:40). Ellicott recalls "they had once before made a like attempt, and had been baffled" (John 7:44); now, with the Prophet "surrounded by His own disciples, and by an admiring crowd," they dared not use "open violence" and "had to fall back upon the more crooked paths of stratagem and treachery."

Clarke draws the moral of such fear-bound restraint: "he who abstains from certain evils, only through fear of scandal or punishment, has already committed them in his heart, and is guilty before God." Against it he sets "the intrepidity of our Lord": "in the very face of his most inveterate enemies, he bears a noble testimony to the truth, reproves their iniquities, denounces the Divine judgments, and... braves danger and death. A true minister of Christ fears nothing but God, when his glory is concerned; a hireling fears every thing, except Him whom he ought to fear."

Clarke also turns the close of the chapter to defend Christ against the charge of secular ambition: through these five days in Jerusalem "he spent in teaching, precisely in the same way he had done for three years past," lodging nightly at Olivet, taking no notice of the people's royal honours "farther than to show that an important prophecy was thus fulfilled" (Isaiah 62:11; Zechariah 9:9). His aim throughout, the witnesses agree, was "to call the people back to purity and holiness" — and so the chapter ends with the King unharmed, the rulers driven to plot in secret, and the conflict of the last week fully joined.
